_meta.issue: #122 [M16] Splitting the Holak Scale diagnosis — ORGANIZATIONAL dimension (v2.1e)
_meta.interaction: Organizational maturity self-check (kind: maturity-check) — NON-gating, neutral framing
_meta.source: AI adoption maturity model „Holak Scale”: organizational dimension v2.1e (organization, 0–11). Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL self-check statements are SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). An indicative diagnosis — it cannot be „failed”. Scoring heuristic is simplified (count of checks → level 0–11) and subject to subject-matter-expert (SME) review.

intro: The Holak Scale's organizational dimension (v2.1e) helps you name AI adoption maturity in your team or company — where the organization is today and what the realistic next step is. It's a self-check, not a test: there is no „pass/fail” result, just an indicative level 0–11 and a hint on how to move up. You can take it independently of the personal diagnosis (MSHP).

screens.title: What the organizational Holak Scale is

blocks.text: The organizational version (v2.1e) describes AI adoption maturity in a team/company across 12 levels (0–11) — from grassroots experiments to conscious, measured, continuously improved adoption embedded in strategy. It helps avoid two traps: „we have AI, so we're mature” and „it's just a toy”.

items: You assess AI adoption in the ORGANIZATION — regardless of any single person's maturity.
An organization may be higher or lower than its best practitioner.
You take the diagnosis of your own practice (Holak Scale v2.1p) separately in module MSHP.

blocks.title: Where the name and the model come from
blocks.text: The Holak Scale was devised by Grzegorz Holak with support from Konrad Gomulski — hence the name. Both are Senior Test Development Engineers at Sii Poland and members of SJSI, and co-founders of quality-blog.eu; Grzegorz Holak is additionally a Test Architect and AI Ambassador at SCIB. We use it as a self-reflection tool, not a certification.

screens.title: How to tell the level: axes and two thresholds

blocks.text: You recognize an organization's maturity not by the number of licenses, but by whether AI is wired into processes: a written acceptable-use policy, permitted uses, mandatory verification of outputs in critical processes, measuring impact on quality and time, and reversible decisions with an audit trail.

items: Barrier — what the organization lacks to move up.
Success — what truly works once the level is reached.
Trap — treating maturity as a finished state rather than continuous improvement.

blocks.title: Two critical thresholds (organizational)
blocks.text: Threshold 8→9: from „individual good practices” to wiring AI into a process with a clear assistant/decision-maker split and an audit trail. Threshold 10→11: from one-off improvements to continuous improvement and calibration of adoption as part of strategy.

screens.title: How to read the organization's result

blocks.text: Next you'll check the statements that are true today for your organization. The number of checks gives an indicative level 0–11 plus a „where you are / how to move up” hint. Check honestly only what is actually in place — not what is planned.

blocks.title: It's a diagnosis, not a grade
blocks.text: There are no correct or incorrect answers here and no pass threshold. The result does not affect path completion or any test — it's a mirror, not an audit.

interaction.title: Holak Scale v2.1e — organizational maturity self-check
interaction.intro: Check the statements true today for your team/company. After checking you'll see an indicative level 0–11 and a next step — with no pass/fail grading.

scales.label: Organization (Holak Scale v2.1e)
scales.intro: How mature is AI adoption in your team/company? Check what is actually in place.

statements.text: Some people use AI for individual tasks.
There is informal sharing of good AI-usage practices.
An acceptable-use policy for AI has been written (data, privacy, IP).
Selected processes have defined, permitted AI uses.
AI outputs in critical processes are mandatorily verified by a human.
Teams measure AI's impact on quality and time, not just the fact of usage.
There is conscious management of context and token cost in repeatable tasks.
AI-supported decisions are reversible and leave an audit trail.
AI is wired into processes with a clear split: assistant vs decision-maker.
The organization regularly calibrates and audits its own AI use.
AI adoption is part of strategy and continuous improvement, not a one-off initiative.

bands.label: Level 0–2: Grassroots experiments
bands.advice: Barrier: no shared rules or goal. Next step: write a simple AI acceptable-use policy (data, privacy) and pick 1–2 pilot processes.

bands.label: Level 3–5: First frameworks
bands.advice: Success: there are rules and permitted uses. Next step: introduce mandatory verification of outputs in critical processes and start measuring impact.

bands.label: Level 6–8: Conscious processes
bands.advice: Success: you measure impact and manage context. Threshold 8→9: wire AI into a process with a clear assistant/decision-maker split and an audit trail for decisions.

bands.label: Level 9–11: Mature adoption
bands.advice: Trap: treating maturity as a finished state rather than continuous improvement. Threshold 10→11: make calibration and auditing of AI use part of strategy and team routine.

points: The Holak Scale v2.1e measures whether AI is wired into processes (rules, verification, measurement), not the number of licenses.
You assess adoption in the organization — your own practice is diagnosed separately (MSHP).
It's an indicative diagnosis: no pass/fail, no effect on completion — it's there to point out the next step.
checklist: Have you named the organization's indicative level (0–11)?
Do you know the nearest barrier to overcome?
Do you know one concrete next step to move up?
summary.nextStep: Next step: pick one barrier from a higher level and plan a single small, reversible adoption step.
